module.title: Compression
module.description: Go beyond the basics — understand compressor types and their characters, shape transients, use parallel compression, glue buses, and train your ear to hear what compression is actually doing.

lessons.title: Compression Types and Characters
lessons.body:
Not all compressors sound the same. Different compressor circuit designs — VCA, FET, optical, variable-mu, and digital — each have distinct characteristics that affect how they respond to transients, how much harmonic colour they add, and how natural or aggressive the compression sounds. Knowing these characters helps you choose the right tool for each job.

## Why Compressor Types Differ

In the analogue era, compression was achieved through different circuit topologies, each with its own electrical behaviour and timing imprecision. Those imprecisions became the sound. Digital compressors model these behaviours — some add the harmonic colouration and non-linear response of the original hardware, others are transparent and clinical.

FL Studio's Fruity Limiter is a clean digital compressor — excellent for control, minimal in colour. Third-party compressor plugins often model specific hardware types for colour and character.

## VCA Compressors

**Character**: Fast, punchy, versatile. Minimal colouration. Precise and predictable.

**Behaviour**: VCA (voltage-controlled amplifier) compressors respond very quickly and consistently. They can have extremely fast attack and release times, making them excellent for controlling transients precisely.

**Sound**: Clean with a slight hardness. The compression is obvious when pushed but not particularly warm or musical.

**Typical uses**: Drums, bass, bus compression, anywhere you want tight control without added colour. The SSL G-Bus compressor — a famous VCA design — is the most iconic bus compressor in pop and hip-hop production.

**FL Studio equivalent**: Fruity Limiter in COMP mode behaves most like a clean VCA — fast, controllable, transparent.

## FET Compressors

**Character**: Aggressive, fast, forward. Adds harmonic saturation at higher compression amounts.

**Behaviour**: FET (field-effect transistor) compressors are even faster than VCA in some designs. They impart a distinctive forward, punchy quality and add upper harmonic content at higher gain reduction levels.

**Sound**: Punchy and slightly aggressive. Higher ratios and fast settings add a pleasant saturation character — the compression becomes part of the tone, not just control.

**Typical uses**: Snares, lead vocals, electric bass, anything that benefits from added presence and attack. The UA 1176 is the archetypal FET compressor.

**Practical note**: When using a plugin modelling a FET compressor, push the input gain higher than you normally would — the saturation and character emerge at higher drive levels.

## Optical Compressors

**Character**: Smooth, natural, slow. Transparent at low to moderate settings.

**Behaviour**: Optical compressors use a light source and a photocell to detect and respond to the signal. The photocell has an inherent, non-linear release time — it recovers slowly at first, then faster — which sounds extremely natural and musical.

**Sound**: Gentle and transparent. The compression feels like the sound is breathing rather than being controlled. Hard to hear as compression; easy to hear as a better-sounding source.

**Typical uses**: Vocals, acoustic instruments, bass, mix buses. The LA-2A is the iconic optical compressor — used on almost every major record of the past 50 years for its musical, hands-off character.

**In FL Studio**: There is no native optical emulation; this is where third-party plugins earn their cost for vocal production.

## Variable-Mu Compressors

**Character**: Warm, slow, and wide. The most musical-sounding compressor type for full-mix use.

**Behaviour**: Variable-mu (also called tube or vari-mu) compressors use vacuum tubes whose gain changes with the input level. They are inherently slow — they cannot catch fast transients — and add significant harmonic warmth.

**Sound**: Thick, warm, gluing. Sounds like the music was recorded together in one room. Transients pass through relatively untouched; sustained content gets compressed gently.

**Typical uses**: Master bus, mix bus, drum room mics. The Fairchild 670 and Manley Vari-Mu are the famous examples. A favourite for adding warmth and cohesion to the full mix without obvious compression.

## Digital / Linear Phase Compressors

**Character**: Transparent and surgical. Exactly what you set is exactly what happens.

**Behaviour**: Digital compressors introduce minimal colouration and respond with perfect accuracy to their parameter settings. Some add zero-latency lookahead (they can react before the transient using buffered processing).

**Sound**: Clean and precise. Can sound clinical or flat if overused.

**Typical uses**: Mastering, podcast production, anywhere transparency matters more than colour. Fruity Limiter is this type.

## Choosing by Task

| Task | Character to Reach For |
|---|---|
| Drum transient control | VCA (fast, precise) |
| Snare punch and bite | FET (aggressive, forward) |
| Vocal smoothing | Optical (natural, transparent) |
| Full mix warmth and glue | Variable-mu (slow, warm) |
| Master bus peak control | Digital / Limiter (transparent) |
| Bass consistency | VCA or optical |

In FL Studio's stock plugin set, Fruity Limiter covers the VCA/digital territory well. For optical and FET characters, budget plugins like TDR Kotelnikov (transparent bus compressor) or Molot (warm VCA-ish character) are free and high quality.
keyTakeaways: VCA compressors are fast and precise with minimal colour — ideal for drums and bus control. FET compressors add aggressive punch and harmonic saturation — ideal for snares and leads. Optical compressors are smooth and natural — ideal for vocals and bass.
Variable-mu compressors are too slow to catch transients but add warmth and glue across sustained content — they are the classic full-mix bus and master bus tool for cohesion.

Fruity Limiter behaves like a clean digital/VCA compressor — excellent for control and transparency. For optical warmth or FET aggression on vocals or individual sources, a third-party plugin is worth the investment.
lessons.tryThis: Open two instances of Fruity Limiter on your snare track. Set both to 4:1 ratio and −18 dB threshold. On the first, set attack to 1 ms (fast — catching the transient immediately). On the second, set attack to 50 ms (slow — letting the transient through). Bypass one at a time and listen to how fast attack dulls the snap of the snare while slow attack preserves it. This attack timing behaviour is what separates VCA-style control from optical-style naturalness — the same compressor, two different characters based on one parameter.

lessons.title: Transient Shaping
lessons.body:
Every sound has two parts: the attack (the initial transient — the sharp spike at the start of the note) and the sustain (everything that follows). Transient shaping is the direct manipulation of these two stages — independently of dynamics or volume. It lets you make a kick punch harder without making it louder, or make a snare tail longer without adjusting compression.

## Why Transients Matter

Transients are what the brain uses to identify and locate sounds. A sharp transient makes a drum sound tight and close — you hear it as a hit. A soft transient makes it sound distant and roomy. Too much transient on every element creates a harsh, exhausting mix. Too little transient makes a mix feel dull and distant.

Most mixing problems described as 'lacking punch' or 'sounding muddy' are transient problems — the attacks are too soft, too consistent, or too buried under sustain.

## Transient Shaping vs Compression

Compression controls dynamic range by turning the level down when the signal exceeds a threshold. It affects transients as a side effect of that level control.

Transient shaping works differently — it directly increases or decreases the attack portion and the sustain portion of a sound, without reference to level thresholds. A transient shaper asks: *do you want more or less attack? more or less sustain?* and applies that directly.

| Tool | Mechanism | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| Compressor | Threshold-based level reduction | Dynamic control, glue |
| Transient shaper | Direct attack/sustain manipulation | Punch, tightness, room control |

## Fruity Peak Controller as a Transient Envelope

FL Studio does not include a dedicated transient shaper plugin. The closest native approach uses **Fruity Peak Controller** or **envelope follower** techniques — or you can achieve similar results with compression attack/release settings:

**Adding attack (more punch)**:
- Use a compressor with a slow attack (40–80 ms) so the transient passes through uncompressed before gain reduction kicks in. The transient is preserved; the sustain is reduced. Result: more perceived punch.

**Reducing attack (softening transients)**:
- Use a compressor with a very fast attack (1–5 ms). The compressor catches the transient immediately and reduces it. This is how to tame a snare that is too aggressive or a hi-hat that is too clickey.

**Adding sustain (longer tail)**:
- Slow release on a compressor allows the sustain portion to stay at a higher level after the transient peak subsides. The tail feels longer and fuller.

**Reducing sustain (tighter sound)**:
- Fast release on a compressor lets the gain return to normal quickly. Combined with a moderate ratio, this tightens the sound — each hit feels discrete and punchy rather than smearing into the next.

## Transient Shaping with Third-Party Plugins

Dedicated transient shapers (Waves Smack Attack, Slate Digital Fresh Air, TDR Kotelnikov's transient response, or the free MSED/Transient Master equivalents) give you direct attack and sustain knobs:

- **Attack knob positive**: Boosts the initial transient — more punch and click
- **Attack knob negative**: Reduces the transient — softer, more distant hit
- **Sustain knob positive**: Extends the decay — more room, more body
- **Sustain knob negative**: Cuts the sustain — tighter, drier sound

## Practical Transient Decisions by Instrument

**Kick drum**:
- More attack: the click at 3–5 kHz is what cuts through on small speakers — if the kick disappears in the mix, add attack
- Less sustain: a boomy kick with too much sustain clutters the bass range — reduce sustain to tighten

**Snare**:
- More attack: makes the snap and crack more prominent — useful if the snare feels buried
- Less attack: tames an overly aggressive or sharp snare that causes fatigue
- More sustain: adds a longer snare ring that sounds more live and roomy

**Hi-hats and cymbals**:
- Less attack: removes the harsh initial tick from a metallic hi-hat sample
- Less sustain: reduces the wash and bleed between hits — tighter, more defined patterns

**Bass / 808**:
- More attack: emphasises the initial pluck or sub hit — more defined note starts
- Less sustain: tightens a sub that rings too long after each note, creating pitch clashes

## The Timing Relationship

Transient shaping interacts with the tempo and feel of the track. Short attacks and short sustains feel tight and modern (trap, electronic music). Long sustains and softer attacks feel loose and organic (lo-fi, jazz, soul). Neither is correct — choose based on the genre feel you are building.
keyTakeaways: Slow compressor attack (40–80 ms) lets the transient pass through before gain reduction engages — this preserves punch. Fast attack (1–5 ms) catches and reduces the transient — this softens a too-aggressive hit.
Most 'lacking punch' mix problems are transient problems — the attacks are buried under sustain or competing with other transients. Address the transient directly before reaching for volume or EQ.

Fast release tightens a sound by letting the compressor recover quickly between hits; slow release extends the sustain tail by keeping the gain reduced longer — use these intentionally to control the feel of rhythmic elements.
lessons.tryThis: On your kick drum, add Fruity Limiter in COMP mode. Set ratio to 4:1, threshold to −18 dB. Now try two extreme attack settings back to back: set attack to 1 ms and listen — the kick loses its initial punch and sounds dull. Then set attack to 60 ms and listen — the transient snaps through before compression engages and the kick hits harder. Settle on the attack time that gives you the punch you want, then adjust release to control how tight or sustained the kick body feels. Toggle the plugin on and off at each setting to confirm what the compressor is actually contributing.

lessons.title: Parallel Compression
lessons.body:
Parallel compression — also called New York compression — solves a specific problem: you want the density and sustain that heavy compression provides, but every time you compress hard enough to get it, you lose the transients that make drums feel alive. Parallel compression keeps both. You blend a heavily compressed copy of the signal underneath the uncompressed original, getting density without sacrificing punch.

## The Problem Parallel Compression Solves

Serial (normal insert) compression is a compromise. A ratio of 2:1 with 3 dB of reduction gives you subtle control but minimal density. A ratio of 10:1 with 15 dB of reduction gives you enormous density and sustain but kills the transients — the kick no longer hits, it thuds. The snare no longer snaps, it thumps.

Parallel compression bypasses this compromise by using two signal paths:

**Path A — Dry**: The original, uncompressed signal. All transients intact.
**Path B — Wet**: Heavily compressed version. Maximum density and sustain.

Blend B underneath A. The transients from path A land cleanly on top of the sustain and density from path B. You hear the best of both.

## Setting Up Parallel Compression in FL Studio

**Method 1 — Send/Return (recommended)**

1. Create a new mixer track labelled 'Parallel Comp'
2. On the source track (e.g. drum bus), hold Alt and click the send knob for the Parallel Comp track — this creates a pre-fader parallel send that stays consistent regardless of fader position
3. Add Fruity Limiter to the Parallel Comp track in COMP mode
4. Set heavy compression: ratio 8:1 to 20:1, fast attack (5–10 ms), medium release (100–200 ms), threshold low enough to get 15–20 dB of gain reduction
5. Add make-up gain to bring the output level up to roughly match the source
6. Blend the Parallel Comp fader under the source — start around −10 dB and raise until you feel the density adding without hearing the compression

**Method 2 — Duplicate track**

Duplicate the mixer track. Route the original to your bus as normal. Add the heavy compressor only to the duplicate. Blend the duplicate under the original with its fader. Simpler to set up but less flexible than the send/return approach.

## The Right Compression Settings for Parallel

Parallel compression works specifically because the compressed signal is blended *under* the original, not replacing it. This means you can use settings that would sound terrible as an insert:

- **Ratio**: 8:1 to 20:1 (or even infinity — full limiting)
- **Attack**: 5–15 ms (fast enough to catch peaks and create density)
- **Release**: 80–200 ms — fast enough to pump with the tempo
- **Threshold**: Very low — you want constant, aggressive compression
- **Gain reduction**: 15–25 dB — the compression is violent in solo, invisible in blend

The heavily compressed signal sounds terrible in solo — flat, punchy, no transients. That is correct. Its job is to contribute density and sustain as a layer, not to sound good alone.

## How Much Parallel to Blend In

The parallel track should be felt, not heard as a separate element. Raise the parallel fader slowly from silence until:
- Drums feel denser and more energetic
- The overall mix feels punchier
- You can just begin to hear the parallel layer as distinct

Then pull back slightly to the point just before it becomes distinct. That is the correct blend level. If you can clearly hear the parallel compressor as a separate sound, it is too loud.

## Parallel Compression on Individual Instruments

Parallel compression is not only for drums. It is effective on:

**Vocals**: A heavily compressed vocal parallel track under the dry vocal gives consistent, upfront presence in the mix without removing the natural dynamics of the performance. The listener hears a vocal that feels present without sounding processed.

**Bass**: Parallel compression on the bass adds sustain and mid-range presence without squashing the transient of each note. Especially effective on bass with significant note-to-note level variation.

**Full mix bus**: A parallel compressed version of the entire mix blended under the main bus adds energy and density to the overall sound — a technique used in mastering as well as mixing.

## What Parallel Compression Is Not

Parallel compression does not add warmth or tonal character on its own — it adds density and sustain. If the compressed parallel signal sounds harsh or unpleasant even at low blend levels, the issue may be the compressor settings or the source signal rather than the technique. Try a slower attack, a gentler ratio, or different make-up gain before abandoning the setup.
keyTakeaways: Parallel compression blends a heavily compressed copy (8:1–20:1, 15–25 dB reduction) underneath the uncompressed original — the dry signal provides the transients, the compressed signal provides density and sustain.
The parallel compressed signal should sound terrible in solo — flat and crushed — that is correct. Its job is to add invisible density as a blend layer, not to sound good on its own.
Raise the parallel fader until the mix feels denser and more energetic, then pull back just to the point before the compressed layer becomes audible as a distinct element — that is the right blend.
lessons.tryThis: Create a mixer track labelled 'Parallel Comp' and Alt+click the send from your drum bus to it. Add Fruity Limiter set to 20:1 ratio, 5 ms attack, 150 ms release, threshold low enough for 20 dB of gain reduction. Add +20 dB of make-up gain to compensate. Now listen to the parallel track in solo — it should sound flat and aggressively compressed with no transient snap. Good. Blend it back under the drum bus fader, starting at −20 dB and slowly raising. Stop when the drums feel more alive and dense but you cannot hear the parallel layer as a separate thing. That blend point is parallel compression working correctly.

lessons.title: Bus Compression and Glue
lessons.body:
Individual track compression controls a single element. Bus compression does something different — it makes a group of separately processed elements feel like they belong together. This quality is called glue, and it is one of the most important sonic characteristics separating a professional mix from a collection of individually good sounds.

## What Glue Actually Is

Glue is the perception that all the elements in a group are occupying the same acoustic space and responding to each other. A well-glued drum kit sounds like it was played in one room. Unglued drums sound like samples assembled from different sources — technically correct but spatially incoherent.

Glue emerges when a bus compressor responds to the combined signal of the group. When the kick hits, the compressor reacts — and slightly pulls back the snare, hi-hats, and other drums simultaneously. When the snare hits, the compressor reacts and pulls everything back slightly. This shared dynamic response is what the brain interprets as 'the same room' — all elements responding to the same acoustic environment.

## Bus Compression Settings for Glue

Glue compression uses gentle settings — the goal is cohesion, not control:

- **Ratio**: 1.5:1 to 4:1 (gentle — you are not trying to heavily compress)
- **Threshold**: Set so you get 1–4 dB of gain reduction on peaks. If you are getting more than 6 dB consistently, pull the threshold up.
- **Attack**: 10–30 ms — slow enough to let transients through, fast enough to catch peaks
- **Release**: 100–400 ms — set to breathe with the tempo. At 120 BPM, 300–400 ms is a natural breath.
- **Gain reduction**: 2–4 dB on peaks, averaging 1–2 dB over a bar

These settings produce a compressor that is barely audible in solo but creates an obvious sense of cohesion when you toggle it in and out in the full mix.

## The Toggle Test

The correct way to evaluate bus compression:

1. Set the compressor to the settings above
2. Play the full mix (not just the compressed bus in solo)
3. Toggle the bus compressor on and off repeatedly while listening
4. With the compressor on, the bus should feel tighter, more unified, more present
5. With the compressor off, the elements should feel slightly looser and more independent

If you cannot hear a difference, increase the gain reduction slightly or lower the threshold. If the difference sounds like obvious pumping or squashing, reduce the gain reduction and raise the threshold.

## The Drum Bus

The drum bus is the most common and highest-impact place for glue compression. A well-set drum bus compressor makes a programmed beat feel more like a live performance:

**Drum bus starting settings**:
- Ratio: 2:1
- Attack: 15 ms
- Release: 200–300 ms (or set to auto-release if available)
- Threshold: −18 to −20 dB
- Gain reduction: 2–4 dB on the loudest hits
- Make-up gain: compensate for the reduction

After setting the compressor, check the drum bus in the context of the full mix. The kick and snare should feel like they are locked together — the kick does not stick out independently, the snare does not feel disconnected from the kit.

## The Mix Bus (Master Bus Compression)

A gentle compressor on the master bus applies glue to the entire mix. This is separate from the master limiter — it comes before limiting in the chain.

**Master bus compression purpose**:
- Makes all elements of the mix breathe together
- Adds perceived loudness and energy without the harshness of heavy limiting
- Creates the sense that the mix was performed in one space rather than assembled

**Master bus settings** (conservative — start here and adjust):
- Ratio: 1.5:1 to 2:1
- Attack: 30–50 ms
- Release: 200–400 ms
- Gain reduction: 1–2 dB — barely moving
- Make-up gain: compensate

**Warning**: Too much master bus compression collapses the dynamics of the entire mix — transients on drums disappear, the mix stops breathing, and everything sounds flat. Less is always more on the master bus. If you find yourself pushing past 3–4 dB of reduction on the master, fix the individual track and bus levels first.

## Glue Compression vs Limiting

Glue compression and limiting serve different purposes and should both be present on a professional master bus:

| Tool | Position | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Glue compressor | Before limiter | Cohesion, warmth, breathing |
| Limiter | Last in chain | Peak control, loudness ceiling |

The compressor does the musical work; the limiter does the safety and loudness work. Running a limiter in place of a compressor for glue produces a mix that is loud but flat — technically controlled, not musically alive.
keyTakeaways: Glue emerges from shared dynamic response — when the bus compressor reacts to one element (like the kick), it pulls all other elements back simultaneously, making the brain perceive them as occupying the same acoustic space.

Bus compression uses gentle settings — 1.5:1 to 4:1 ratio, 2–4 dB of gain reduction on peaks. Evaluate it by toggling on and off in the full mix, not in solo: cohesion is only audible when all elements are playing together.

Master bus compression (1–2 dB reduction, ratio under 2:1) adds mix cohesion before the limiter — the compressor does the musical work of gluing elements together, the limiter does the safety and loudness work.
lessons.tryThis: Add Fruity Limiter to your drum bus in COMP mode. Set ratio to 2:1, attack to 15 ms, release to 250 ms, and lower the threshold until the gain reduction meter shows 2–3 dB on the loudest hits. Play the full mix and toggle the compressor on and off repeatedly — listen for whether the drums feel more locked together and present with it on. If you cannot hear a difference, lower the threshold by 3 dB and repeat. Once you find the setting where the toggle test reveals clear cohesion without obvious pumping, that is your drum bus glue point. Add make-up gain to match the bypass level before comparing.

lessons.title: Limiting and Loudness
lessons.body:
A limiter is a compressor with a very high ratio — typically 20:1 or higher, often described as infinity:1 — that sets an absolute ceiling on the signal level. Nothing gets through above the ceiling. Limiters serve two purposes in a mix: protecting against digital clipping, and increasing the perceived loudness of a track to competitive streaming levels. Understanding both purposes — and how they interact — is essential before you export anything for release.

## What Limiting Is

When any digital audio signal exceeds 0 dBFS (the maximum digital level), the waveform clips — the top of the peaks are cut off, creating a harsh, distorted sound. A limiter set with a ceiling of −0.3 dBFS ensures no sample ever reaches or exceeds that level regardless of dynamics in the mix.

Beyond clipping prevention, limiting is the primary tool for loudness. By catching the loudest peaks and pulling them down, you create headroom that you can fill by raising the overall level — the mix gets louder because the peaks are no longer hitting the ceiling.

## The Loudness-Dynamics Trade-off

Every dB of perceived loudness gained through limiting costs dynamic range. A mix with a limiter doing 6 dB of peak reduction and 6 dB of compensating output gain sounds 6 dB louder, but the distance between the loudest and quietest moments is 6 dB smaller. Push too hard and the mix sounds flat — every moment is equally loud, nothing has impact because nothing has contrast.

```
Light limiting:   Peak → -2 dBTP   LUFS: -14  Dynamics: present, punchy
Moderate:         Peak → -1 dBTP   LUFS: -10  Dynamics: slightly reduced
Heavy limiting:   Peak → -0.3 dBTP LUFS: -7   Dynamics: flattened, fatiguing
```

## LUFS: The Loudness Metric That Matters

Peak level (dBFS) measures the highest instantaneous sample. LUFS (Loudness Units relative to Full Scale) measures the perceived loudness over time — averaged across the full duration of a track. Streaming platforms normalise to LUFS targets, not peak targets.

**Streaming platform targets** (integrated LUFS):

| Platform | Target LUFS | True Peak Max |
|---|---|---|
| Spotify | −14 LUFS | −1 dBTP |
| Apple Music | −16 LUFS | −1 dBTP |
| YouTube | −14 LUFS | −1 dBTP |
| Tidal | −14 LUFS | −1 dBTP |
| SoundCloud | −14 LUFS | −1 dBTP |

If your track exceeds the target, the platform turns it down automatically. If it is below target, some platforms turn it up, some leave it quiet. Mastering to approximately −14 LUFS integrated is a safe universal target — competitive loudness on all platforms without being penalised.

## Fruity Limiter: The LIMIT Tab

In the LIMIT tab of Fruity Limiter:

- **Ceiling** (the topmost knob): The absolute maximum output level. Set to −0.3 dBFS or −1.0 dBFS. Never 0 dBFS — even a hair above 0 in the digital domain causes inter-sample peaks that clip after format conversion.
- **Release**: How quickly the limiter recovers after a peak. Too fast causes distortion (the limiter opens and closes audibly). Too slow reduces average loudness and dulls transients. Start at 300–500 ms.
- **Gain** (the large knob feeding into the limiter): The amount of drive going into the limiter. Increase this to push the limiter harder and raise the perceived loudness. Watch the gain reduction meter.

## A Practical Limiting Workflow

1. Add Fruity Limiter to the master bus in LIMIT mode, after any glue compressor
2. Set ceiling to −0.3 dBFS
3. Set release to 400 ms
4. Increase the input gain slowly until the loudest sections of the track show 2–4 dB of gain reduction on the meter
5. Export a test WAV and check the LUFS in a metering plugin or the exported file properties
6. Adjust input gain until the integrated LUFS is at your target (−14 LUFS for streaming)
7. Listen for artefacts — pumping, dulled transients, distorted low end — and back off if present

## True Peak vs Sample Peak

Sample peak measures the highest digital sample value. True peak (dBTP) accounts for inter-sample peaks — the actual signal level in the analogue domain after digital-to-analogue conversion, which can exceed the sample peak due to reconstruction filtering. Set your limiter ceiling to −0.3 dBTP (not just dBFS) for maximum safety across all playback systems.

## How Much Is Too Much Limiting

Signs of overlimiting:
- Drums lose punch — the kick and snare no longer hit, they just exist at a consistent level
- The mix sounds fatiguing after 30 seconds
- The gain reduction meter shows more than 6 dB constantly (not just on peaks)
- Bass notes and 808s distort at the limiter
- The mix sounds noticeably worse than the reference track even at the same LUFS level

The solution to overlimiting is always to fix the mix first — better gain staging, better EQ, better compression on individual tracks — so the master limiter has less work to do. A well-mixed track rarely needs more than 3–4 dB of peak limiting to reach −14 LUFS.
keyTakeaways: Set the limiter ceiling to −0.3 dBFS (never 0) — inter-sample peaks during format conversion can exceed sample peak values, and headroom below 0 dBFS prevents clipping on all playback systems.
Master to −14 LUFS integrated for streaming: this is a safe universal target that avoids loudness normalisation penalties on Spotify, Apple Music, and YouTube simultaneously.

Signs of overlimiting — kick loses punch, mix fatigues in 30 seconds, constant gain reduction above 6 dB — always point back to the mix: better gain staging and EQ reduce how hard the limiter has to work.
lessons.tryThis: Add Fruity Limiter to your master bus in LIMIT mode. Set the ceiling to −0.3 dBFS and release to 400 ms. Slowly increase the input gain knob until you see 2–4 dB of gain reduction on the loudest sections. Export a 30-second section as a WAV. Check the perceived loudness — does it feel competitive with a reference track at the same volume? Now push the input gain until the meter shows 10+ dB of constant reduction. Export that version and compare. Hear how the heavily limited version sounds louder at first but fatiguing within seconds, while the lightly limited version retains punch and dynamics. Find the setting where loudness and dynamics coexist.

lessons.title: Compression by Ear
lessons.body:
Every lesson in this module has given you parameters — numbers for ratio, attack, release, threshold. Those numbers are starting points. The real skill is hearing what compression is doing and adjusting by what you hear rather than what you see. This lesson trains that skill directly.

## The Problem with Parameter-First Thinking

Beginners set a ratio of 4:1, an attack of 10 ms, and a release of 200 ms because those are 'correct' starting values. Then they move on. They never listen to whether the compressor is helping, hurting, or doing nothing at all.

Experienced engineers set approximate starting values, then move each parameter until the sound changes in the direction they want — and stop when it does. The parameter values are a result of listening, not a substitute for it.

## Training Exercise 1: Hear the Threshold

The threshold determines when compression starts. Too high and nothing gets compressed. Too low and everything gets squashed constantly.

**Exercise**: Set ratio to 4:1, attack to 20 ms, release to 200 ms. Set the threshold all the way up (no compression). Slowly lower the threshold while watching the gain reduction meter and listening.

- At first: nothing changes — the threshold is still above the signal
- When the meter starts moving: you are beginning to compress
- As you lower further: hear the dynamics reduce — loud moments get pulled toward the average level
- Too low: the compressor never fully releases, the sound loses all dynamics and feels flat

Target: the threshold where you can just hear the compressor starting to act. That is your starting point for this source.

## Training Exercise 2: Hear the Attack

**Exercise**: Set a moderate ratio (4:1) and threshold that gives 4–6 dB of reduction on peaks. Set attack to 1 ms. Play the source in a loop.

- At 1 ms: the compressor reacts immediately. Hear how the initial snap of a snare, the click of a kick, or the pluck of a bass disappears — the transient is caught and compressed.
- Slowly increase attack to 10 ms, 30 ms, 60 ms, 100 ms. At each step, the transient passes through longer before compression engages.
- At 60–100 ms: the full transient is clearly present, only the sustain is being controlled.

The correct attack is the setting where the transient has the character you want. If you want punch, use a slower attack. If you want smoothness or the transient is too harsh, use a faster attack.

## Training Exercise 3: Hear the Release

**Exercise**: Keep the same ratio and threshold. Set attack to 30 ms. Now vary the release.

- At 50 ms release: the compressor recovers very quickly. On a rhythmic source, you will hear pumping — the compressor opens and closes audibly with each hit, creating a swelling quality.
- At 200 ms: smoother recovery. Less pumping, more natural breathing.
- At 600 ms: slow recovery. The compressor stays partly engaged between hits — sustained reduction that removes dynamics and feels thick or sluggish.

The musical release is the setting where the compressor breathes with the tempo. As a rough guide: the compressor should recover most of the way to unity before the next loud hit arrives. At 120 BPM, one beat is 500 ms — a release of 300–400 ms recovers most of the way in time.

## Training Exercise 4: Hear the Ratio

**Exercise**: Set threshold so you have moderate reduction (6–8 dB). Now vary the ratio while listening.

- At 1.5:1: barely audible compression. The dynamic range is barely reduced.
- At 4:1: obvious control. Loud moments are noticeably pulled toward the average.
- At 8:1: heavy. The signal above the threshold is strongly controlled — dense, controlled sound.
- At 20:1+: limiting. The signal barely exceeds the threshold ceiling.

Notice that the ratio changes the *character* of the compression as much as the amount. Higher ratios sound more aggressive and controlled; lower ratios sound transparent and musical.

## The A/B Habit

Every compression decision should end with an A/B test:

1. Set make-up gain so the bypassed and compressed versions are at the same level
2. Toggle the compressor on and off in the context of the full mix
3. Ask: is the compressed version better? In what specific way? Or is it just different?

If you cannot clearly state why the compressed version is better — more controlled transients, more consistent dynamics, more sustain, more glue — consider whether the compressor is actually helping.

## The Ear-First Workflow

Once you have the ear-training foundation:

1. Start with: ratio 4:1, attack 20 ms, release 200 ms, threshold to taste
2. Ask: what do I want this compression to do? Control transients? Add sustain? Glue?
3. Adjust attack until the transient character is right
4. Adjust release until the recovery feels musical
5. Adjust threshold until the amount of compression is appropriate
6. Adjust ratio for the character — gentle or aggressive
7. A/B in the full mix. If it is better, keep it. If it is not clearly better, remove it.

Compression that is not clearly improving the sound is adding noise, phase shift, and processing overhead. Remove it.
keyTakeaways: Train attack by ear: slow attack (60–100 ms) lets the full transient through before compression engages; fast attack (1–5 ms) catches and smooths the transient. The correct setting is whichever transient character serves the mix.

Train release by ear: the compressor should recover most of the way to unity before the next loud hit arrives. At 120 BPM (500 ms per beat), a release of 300–400 ms breathes with the tempo without audible pumping.

If you cannot clearly state why the compressed version is better than the bypassed version — after level-matching — remove the compressor. Compression that does not clearly improve the sound only adds processing overhead.
lessons.tryThis: Pick one instrument — the snare is ideal for this exercise. Add Fruity Limiter in COMP mode and set ratio 4:1, attack 20 ms, release 200 ms, threshold at −20 dB. Now do all four training exercises in order: (1) move only the threshold and hear where compression starts; (2) move only the attack from 1 ms to 100 ms and hear the transient change; (3) move only the release from 50 ms to 600 ms and hear the pumping and breathing; (4) move only the ratio from 1.5:1 to 20:1 and hear the character shift. After each exercise, set the parameter to what sounds best to you — not what the 'correct' number is. End with an A/B toggle in the full mix and confirm it is genuinely better.
